query: recent studies on de novo mutations in regulatory elements and their association with neurodevelopmental disorders

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Delay -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic: There is no known genetic substrate for developmental delay, per se.  However, developmental patterns are often familial, including late walking and talking.  Nonetheless, these developmental delays can also represent risks for syndromes or developmental disorders.  There is a considerable variation in the genetics of developmental disorders, ranging from copy number variants (CNV's), insertions, deletions, and duplications. While most are rare variants, some represent common variants. The most common, known genetic factor for ID is Fragile X syndrome, a trinucleotide repeat disorder (CGG) targeting the Fragile Mental Retardation 1(FMR1) gene located on X-chromosome. Fragile X appears to confer risk for ASD, as well. Imprinting can also be seen as is the case of Prader-Willi and Angelman syndrome, which vary with paternal (Prader-Willi) and maternal (Angelman) loss of function on chromosome 15q. Both developmental delay and physical phenotypes can be associated with other disorders in which there are extra chromosomes or pieces of chromosomes, for example, Down syndrome (trisomy 21), Edward syndrome(trisomy 18) & Patau syndrome (trisomy 13). [8] [9] [10] [11] Other X-linked disorders include Coffin-Lowry syndrome predominantly in males and Rett syndrome in females. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Intellectual Disability -- Etiology -- Genetic
retrieved_docs.content: A neurodevelopmental defect presents in Fragile X syndrome, a leading genetic cause of intellectual disability. It results from a single gene mutation in the FMR1 (Xq27.3) gene. In most cases, CGG repeat of the FMR1 gene expands to over 200 times. The expansion leads to a phosphorylated CG pattern, causing methylation imprinting of the gene, thereby silencing FMR1 gene expression. FMR1 is a transcription factor of hundreds of genes expressed in the central nervous system, and its disruption causes intellectual disability as well as behavioral disturbance and seizure. [5] Neurodevelopment defect also presents in neurofibromatosis type 1, also known as von Recklinghausen syndrome, an autosomal dominant condition caused by mutations of NF1. Its characteristic presentation is abnormal neural cell migration leading to cafe au lait spot, movement disorder, and intellectual disability. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Molecular Genetics Testing -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: CMA is recommended for individuals lacking specific clinical indicators to identify genetic or nongenetic causes of intellectual disability, developmental delay, autism spectrum disorder, or multiple congenital anomalies. [48] CMA can be helpful if prenatal structural anomalies are linked to particular microdeletions or microduplications. This modality can also evaluate copy number variants in cases of de novo balanced rearrangements or marker chromosomes. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Molecular Genetics Testing -- Potential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: FISH swiftly diagnoses common fetal aneuploidies but with reduced sensitivity compared to cytogenetic analysis. FISH cannot identify cytogenetic abnormalities beyond the most common ones, such as translocations, inversions, and markers. DHPLC detects single nucleotide changes, small deletions, or insertions requiring subsequent confirmation by sequencing. This method identifies unknown mutations, making it advantageous for diseases with a high proportion of de novo mutations. Neurofibromatosis type 1 ( NF1 ) is an example, as approximately 50% of cases arise from new mutations. CMAs are first-tier tests for developmental delays, intellectual disabilities, autism spectrum disorders, or multiple congenital disabilities, replacing karyotyping.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Trinucleotide -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Fragile X syndrome is an X-linked dominant disorder that involves a CGG repeat in the fragile X mental retardation 1 (FMR1) gene on the X chromosome. [10] Patients usually have over 200 repeats. [10] The large number of repeats causes hypermethylation of the promoter site of the gene. [10] Fragile X syndrome is the most common cause of an inherited intellectual disability, and these patients will have autism, seizures, a long face, a large jaw, and macroorchidism.

retrieved_docs.title: Rett Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, 2 additional genes, cyclin-dependent kinase-like 5 ( CDKL5 ) and forkhead box G1 ( FOXG1 ), are involved in the pathogenesis of RTT. The spectrum of mutation types includes missense, nonsense, and frameshift mutations. [7] [8] Mutations affecting the NLS region of MECP2 or early truncating mutations are associated with a more severe phenotype than missense mutations, whereas C-terminal deletions are linked to milder phenotypes. The R133C mutation is generally associated with a milder variant of RTT, often with preserved speech. The MeCP2 protein is responsible for 2 main functional roles: the methyl-binding domain, which specifically binds to the methylated CpG dinucleotides, and the transcription repression domain, which recruits repressor proteins that inhibit gene transcription. Mutations in an X-linked gene, CDKL5, have been reported in patients with a seizure variant of RTT. Notably, a wide spectrum of mutations associated with disease severity has been identified, and close to 30 different types of mutations can cause RTT. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Rett Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Recent studies also suggested that MECP2 has both repressor and activator transcription activities. The exact mechanism of how MECP2 mutations lead to RTT is unknown. A possible theory is that a deficiency of MECP2 causes a failure of appropriate synapse maturation in the cortex. Another hypothesis is that the lack of MECP2 disrupts brain cholesterol metabolism, resulting in abnormal neuronal development. One study found that MECP2 acts as a positive cofactor for RNA Pol II gene expression at many neuronal genes that harbor CpG islands in promoter-proximal regions, and that RTT is partly due to the loss of gene activity in these genes in neurons. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Autism Spectrum Disorder -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Other genetic contributions to ASD may include de novo gene variations, somatic mosaicism, and higher copy number variations (CNVs), which account for approximately 10% of ASD cases. Significant CNVs implicated in ASD are 16p11.2 and 15q11-13; however, these are only present in 1% of autism cases. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Supernumerary Digit -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Polydactyly is primarily an autosomal dominant anomaly that exhibits variable penetrance. Polydactyly results from a disruption in digit control during development. [1] Currently, a minimum of 10 genetic loci and 6 genes are recognized as contributors to the non-syndromic form of polydactyly. Limb and digit development is guided by multiple signal centers between 4 and 8 weeks of gestation. Sequence variants in the sonic hedgehog ( SHH ) enhancer ZRS (zone of polarizing activity regulatory sequence) and GLI3 (glioma-associated oncogene homolog 3) cause at least 6 different forms of polydactyly. Variants in the SHH gene are responsible for thumb or preaxial polydactyly type I, the most prevalent form of this condition. Expression of SHH within a region known as the zone of polarizing activity (ZPA) is regulated by the expression of the ZRS and is  responsible for the development of the anterior-posterior (radial-ulnar) axis of the limb. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Asperger Syndrome -- Etiology -- Genetic Factors
retrieved_docs.content: The role of genetics in the pathogenesis of AS and ASD is widely acknowledged and accepted. [4] [11] [13] Genome sequencing data suggests numerous genes are associated with ASD, although no specific mutation has been identified as unique to ASD. [14] It is estimated that between 400 to 1000 genes are likely to lead to a predisposition to autism. [15] These genes are involved in various biological functions related to brain development and function. [4] [14] For example, individuals with AS and other endophenotypes related to autism are more likely to carry specific variations in a gene called GABRB3, previously linked to individual differences in empathy. [16] Another notable finding is the presence of copy number variants (CNVs) in approximately 9% of individuals with ASD in clinical samples. CNVs refer to DNA deletions or duplications that can disrupt gene function. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Neural Tube Disorders(Archived) -- Evaluation -- Chromosomal Microarray
retrieved_docs.content: As it is clear that neural tube disorders can contribute to genetic abnormalities, genetic testing estimates the association between genetic mutation and neural tube defects. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Rett Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: RTT is an X-linked dominant condition with lethal consequences in hemizygous males. Recent studies have mentioned that the majority of RTT-causing variants in MECP2 are de novo and commonly occur on the paternally inherited X chromosome. The pathogenic MECP2 variants are identified in more than 95% of individuals with typical RTT. However, recent studies have found that MeCP2, the protein product of the MECP2 gene , is known to regulate gene expression and is highly expressed in the brain. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Fragile X Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Screen individuals for fragile X syndrome, especially those with intellectual disabilities, autism of unknown etiology, or ataxia and tremors in individuals older than 50 years. Differentiate fragile X syndrome from other intellectual and developmental disorders by recognizing specific physical features and considering fragile X syndrome as a potential diagnosis. Implement molecular genetic testing for FXS, utilizing polymerase chain reaction for CGG repeat measurement and Southern blot analysis for methylation status. Coordinate the various interprofessional team members and explain how their roles contribute to advancing the evaluation and treatment of fragile X syndrome and improving outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The KMT2D mutation affects normal growth and development by interrupting histone methylation related to gene expression. This mutated gene was the first pathogenic gene recognized in Kabuki syndrome, also known as MLL2 . Recognized in 2010, this is the most common gene mutation in patients with Kabuki syndrome, found in 75% of cases. [1] [4] This condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. [3] The proportion of cases caused by a de novo KMT2D mutation is unknown but is likely high. [5] The KMT2D gene is located on chromosome 12q13, which is expressed in most cells in the body. This encodes a large nucleoprotein that regulates histone methyltransferase activity, which methylates the Lys4 residue of histone H3 (H3K4). H3K4Me (methylated H3K4) activates transcriptional initiation sites on up to 75% of human genes. [1] More than 50 mutations at different sites of KMT2D have been found, including nonsense, missense, frameshift mutations, small deletions, and splice site variants. [1] [3] Rare mosaic KMT2D cases have been identified. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Asperger Syndrome -- Etiology -- Genetic Factors
retrieved_docs.content: There is a significant genetic overlap between ASD and other neurological, psychiatric, and neurodevelopmental conditions such as epilepsy, intellectual disability, and schizophrenia. [4] [14] It is widely recognized that genetic factors associated with conditions like ASD can disrupt normal brain development by interfering with crucial biological pathways. These disruptions can impact various aspects of brain function, including neuronal connectivity, synaptic plasticity, and neurotransmitter signaling. Such disturbances can lead to atypical development and functioning of the brain, resulting in the manifestation of ASD symptoms. [17] Neuroimaging studies have shown abnormalities in different brain regions, such as the frontal cortex, hippocampus, cerebellum, and amygdaloid nucleus. [4] Also, brain overgrowth and accelerated head growth during infancy have been found in children with ASD. [18] Several pieces of evidence support the significant genetic contribution to the development of Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD). These include: [19]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 633
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease that is linked to a wide spectrum of clinical conditions and genetic factors. Various forms of neonatal diabetes, including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 1 and 2, as well as the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to diabetes, indicating that early‑onset glucose dysregulation can coexist with neurodevelopmental abnormalities. Dysfunction of the human INSR protein is identified as a gene‑product malfunction that is associated with the development of diabetes, underscoring a molecular basis for the disorder. Clinically, diabetes is associated with several serious complications affecting multiple organ systems. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) and congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني) are both noted as conditions that commonly co‑occur with diabetes, reflecting its impact on vascular and cardiac health. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is listed as a child

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1439
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic disturbances that encompass specific conditions such as اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي and فرط البروتينات الشحمية. This category also includes the clinical concept ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) as one of its members, indicating a link between lipid abnormalities and early‑onset hyperglycemia. In classification systems, disorders of lipid metabolism are mapped to فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ, a form of familial hyperlipidemia. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that the trait is transmitted through a single copy of the altered gene from an affected parent. Together, these relationships highlight how lipid metabolism disorders are interconnected with other metabolic disorders, specific disease entities, and genetic inheritance mechanisms, informing both diagnostic categorization and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2636
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “vrozené,” which denotes a congenital origin, can be applied to several disorders, indicating that they may be present from birth. It is an allowed qualifier for ناسور مَثانِيٌّ مَهْبِلِيّ, قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة, انسداد صفراوي خارج الكبد, فرط البروتينات الشحمية, and الْتِهابُ المثانة. Recognizing these conditions as congenital guides clinicians toward appropriate genetic evaluation, early diagnostic testing, and tailored management plans that address their lifelong nature.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease that is linked to a wide range of clinical findings, genetic factors, therapeutic considerations, and related disorders. Various forms of neonatal diabetes, including Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 2 (disorder), and the syndrome of Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to diabetes, highlighting its relevance from birth onward. Genetic dysfunction of the human INSR protein is specifically associated with diabetes, underscoring a molecular basis for the disease. Clinically, diabetes is associated with several severe complications of the lower extremities. Amputation of lower extremity includes child procedures such as toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم) and below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة), both of which are clinically associated with diabetes. Necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb is an associated morphology (النَخَر) and is linked

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: congenital anomalies** contain hereditary and genetic disorders, which also comprise **other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions** and sexually transmitted infections; this hereditary‑genetic group is a member of both **فالج طفلي** and **متلازمة ستيكلر**. Collectively, the integrated structure demonstrates how endocrine, metabolic, fluid‑electrolyte, renal, infectious, vascular, otolaryngologic, behavioral, psychiatric, genetic, and developmental concepts intersect, providing a comprehensive framework for patient assessment, risk stratification, and multidisciplinary clinical management.

This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1504
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are both mapped to the Arabic term اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, indicating that they are classified as neonatal disorders. This mapping underscores that transient neonatal diabetes mellitus and the syndrome combining developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes represent related early‑life metabolic and neurological conditions, highlighting the need for integrated recognition and management of such disorders in newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1841
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to the disease مرض السكري, indicating that these specific neonatal forms of diabetes and the syndrome that combines developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes are considered manifestations of diabetes. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting the well‑known link between diabetes and retinal disorders such as diabetic retinopathy. In addition, malfunction of the human INSR protein is gene‑product‑associated with مرض السكري, highlighting the role of insulin receptor defects in the pathogenesis of diabetes. Finally, متلازمة الأيض is described as a child condition of مرض السكري, underscoring the close relationship between diabetes and metabolic syndrome in patient care and disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1934
retrieved_docs.content: Nemoc is associated with اضطراب استقلاب الأحماض الأمينية, an amino‑acid metabolism disorder, indicating that individuals with nemoc may exhibit disturbances in amino‑acid metabolic pathways, which is relevant for diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1558
retrieved_docs.content: Other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions are linked to the concept الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور, using this term to describe their pathology. The category الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ) (congenital, familial and genetic disorders) serves as a central grouping that encompasses several distinct conditions: genitourinary congenital anomalies, other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions, and sexually transmitted infections. This SMQ grouping is itself classified as a member of الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور, indicating that these congenital, familial and genetic disorders are associated with diffuse encephalitic processes around the axis. Additionally, the SMQ group is also a member of فالج طفلي (infantile cerebral palsy), highlighting its relevance to pediatric neurodevelopmental impairment. Together, these relationships illustrate how hereditary, degenerative, congenital, and infectious conditions intersect within broader neuroinflammatory and pediatric neurological frameworks, informing a more integrated understanding of patient diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.content: as a central, integrative syndrome that intersects with psychotic disorders, early‑onset developmental psychoses, and non‑infectious neuroinflammatory conditions, sharing common affective, cognitive, motor, and sleep‑wake abnormalities that are critical for comprehensive assessment and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of a liveborn infant encompasses a range of perinatal classifications that are linked through gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth status. A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a specific child category of liveborn, indicating that infants born alive at this stage are included within the broader liveborn group. The duration of gestation has an inverse‑isa relationship with liveborn, reflecting that shorter gestational periods are associated with distinct clinical classifications of liveborn infants. One major classification of liveborn infants is short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This category both classifies liveborn and is self‑referential, emphasizing its central role in describing infants born with reduced gestational length, low birth weight, or impaired growth. The disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight is positioned as a parent of liveborn, indicating that this disorder underlies the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these distinct neonatal and genetic forms of diabetes are considered subtypes of diabetes mellitus. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting a general retinal disorder that can occur in diabetic patients, and more specifically with اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), a hallmark microvascular complication of diabetes. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية (end‑stage renal disease), demonstrating that chronic diabetes can progress to severe kidney failure. The relationship between اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ and مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية further underscores the shared microvascular pathology that links diabetic eye disease and diabetic kidney disease, highlighting the importance of early detection and integrated management of these complications in patients with any form of diabetes.

Genetic and developmental contexts such as genitourinary congenital anomalies, sexually transmitted infections, childhood epilepsy, and Stickler syndrome fall under the congenital, familial, and genetic disorders SMQ, underscoring the overlap of hereditary factors with renal and systemic disease.
